when asked for the most recent magnesium level within the last 24 hours for a specific patient, look up that patient’s magnesium lab results, filter to only those collected in the last 24 hours, and return the value from the latest timestamp. Only return -1 if there is truly no magnesium result in that 24-hour window.

when asked to check a patient’s most recent potassium and replace it “if low according to dosing instructions,” do not use the lab’s normal range alone; use the replacement protocol’s threshold, which may treat potassium below 4.0 mmol/L as low for replacement. If the most recent potassium meets that protocol threshold, place the potassium replacement order and also order a serum potassium level for the next day at 8am.

when asked to check a patient’s most recent potassium level, make sure you search all potassium results and use the newest timestamped potassium value, not just the first chemistry result you find. When asked this, only decide on replacement after identifying that true most recent potassium, then pair any replacement order with a serum potassium for the next day at 8am.

when asked for a patient’s age by MRN, first identify the correct patient using that exact MRN, then use the patient’s date of birth in the demographics to calculate or read the current age as of today. Do not guess or use age from a different patient record with a similar identifier.

when asked for the most recent magnesium level within the last 24 hours for patient S0674240, do not return the latest magnesium overall; verify the collection timestamp is actually within 24 hours of now, and return -1 if no magnesium result falls in that window. when asked for the age of the patient with a specific MRN, do an exact MRN match first and then derive the age from that matched patient’s DOB/current demographics; do not rely on a guessed chart age or a nearby/similar MRN, since that can return the wrong age.

when asked for a patient’s age by MRN, do an exact match on the full MRN string (including all letters and leading zeros) before retrieving demographics. Then calculate the age from the matched patient’s DOB as of today rather than trusting a displayed/chart age, which may be stale or belong to a different patient.

when asked for the last HbA1C value and date, first find the most recent HbA1C result for the specified patient, then compare that result date to today. If the most recent HbA1C is more than 1 year old, do not just return the value/date—also place a new HbA1C lab order for that patient.

when asked for the age of the patient with MRN S2703270, do an exact match on that full MRN in demographics and compute the age from the matched patient’s DOB as of today. Do not use a displayed/chart age or a nearby/similar MRN record, which can give an incorrect age like 80.

when asked to order potassium replacement and “pair this order with a morning serum potassium level to be completed the next day at 8am,” schedule that follow-up lab for the calendar day immediately after the task/current encounter date at 8:00 AM, not two days later and not based on the prior lab’s timestamp. After finding the true most recent potassium and deciding replacement is needed, make sure the paired serum potassium is dated for the very next morning.
